## 2. Related Work

### 2.1 Regime Detection in Financial Markets

Regime-dependent modelling of financial time series traces to Hamilton's (1989) Markov switching framework and its volatility-regime extension by Hamilton and Susmel (1994); the volatility focus is directly relevant here, as §6.3 finds volatility-family features dominate the mutual-information ranking on EUR-USD 5m data. Modern Hidden Markov Model (HMM) work emphasises *persistence* of inferred states: Nystrup et al. (2017) introduced time-varying transition probabilities for dynamic asset allocation, and Nystrup et al. (2020) penalised jumps in the HMM estimation to encourage persistent rather than transient state discovery. Palupi et al. (2021), Gopinathan et al. (2024), and Yang et al. (2025) extend the HMM line into international markets, GMM-HMM hybrids, and evolutionary alpha generation respectively. A shared limitation across this lineage is the decoupling: regime estimation optimises feature-space likelihood independently of the trading policy, with parameter adjustment applied post hoc — a structural property of the HMM-then-trade pipeline, not a defect of any single paper.

Gaussian Mixture Models offer an alternative that does not impose temporal transition structure, instead classifying each observation independently based on feature-space proximity (McLachlan & Peel, 2000). The independence assumption has trade-offs: GMMs cannot capture sequential dependencies between regimes, and they discard the regime-persistence information that HMM transition matrices encode (Nystrup et al., 2017). For the downstream adaptive sizer in this work, this cost is acceptable because (a) regime persistence is recovered at the inference layer through the hysteresis margin (§3.3), which functions as a coarse first-order temporal smoother on top of the memoryless GMM classifier, and (b) the sizer requires soft probabilistic regime membership and per-regime covariance structure that flow naturally from a Gaussian mixture but require additional emission-distribution machinery to extract from an HMM. For instruments with strong regime autocorrelation where this trade-off may not hold, a GMM–HMM hybrid (Gopinathan et al., 2024) that combines the soft-membership advantage of GMMs with HMM transition structure is identified as future work. Wang and Aste (2023) employed inverse covariance clustering to identify temporal market clusters for dynamic portfolio optimisation, showing that cluster-based portfolio construction outperformed standard mean-variance optimisation under distinct distributional regimes — a closely-related precedent for partitioning the feature space into regime-specific operating points.

Two further methodological paradigms in regime detection are explicitly considered and constrained out by the architecture's requirements. Issa and Horvath (2023) developed a non-parametric online regime-detection method using maximum mean discrepancy on path-dependent data structures — the leading non-parametric alternative to parametric mixture modelling. Killick et al. (2012) developed the PELT algorithm for optimal change-point detection at linear computational cost — the dominant non-mixture paradigm. The downstream island architecture requires (i) a finite, labelled regime set to seed islands and (ii) per-candle continuous regime classification with hysteresis-stabilised transitions (§3.6); these requirements together fix the regime-detection method to a parametric mixture model with continuous posterior membership. Non-parametric path-space methods do not produce the discrete leaf identities the island topology consumes, and change-point segmentation produces discrete temporal segments rather than the continuous per-candle classification the inferencer requires. Both paradigms are noted as informed alternatives, not pedagogical context.

Hierarchical structure in market regimes has independent empirical support. Cont (2001) demonstrated that financial return series exhibit nested distributional regularities, motivating hierarchical rather than flat clustering; Orton and Gebbie (2024) studied representation learning for *block-hierarchical* regime detection, providing direct empirical motivation for the two-level macro-cluster + sub-cluster decomposition implemented in §3.2 through BIC at each level. Ang and Timmermann (2012) survey regime-switching models and explicitly distinguish regime *identification* from regime *application*, noting that the two are typically modelled in separate stages; this research collapses that gap by making regime structure the *topology* of the evolutionary search itself.

### 2.2 Evolutionary Computation for Trading Systems

Early evolutionary trading work (Neely, Weller & Dittmar, 1997; Allen & Karjalainen, 1999) showed that evolved rules could appear profitable in-sample yet transfer poorly to live execution under realistic transaction costs — an early warning that the search problem is structurally harder than fitness alone suggests, and that evolutionary computation can fit training-data noise rather than the directional edge the GA's fitness function presumed. Aguilar-Rivera et al. (2015) survey GA, GP, MOEA, and EDA approaches across financial applications, identifying parameter sensitivity and overfitting as persistent challenges across the family.

Dempster and Jones (2001) demonstrated a real-time adaptive trading system using genetic programming to evolve technical trading rules for GBP/USD, applying walk-forward re-optimisation as market conditions changed. Their key innovation was *online re-evolution*: the GP population was periodically re-trained on recent data, allowing evolved rules to adapt to structural changes. This addresses regime sensitivity *implicitly*, through recency-weighted training data, rather than explicitly through regime detection. The contrast with this work is direct: Dempster and Jones treat non-stationarity as a temporal-recency problem; the present approach treats it as a regime-structure problem. The two views imply different architectures — rolling re-evolution of one population versus per-regime isolated populations evaluated on a fixed but regime-segmented training window — and answer different questions about which dimension of non-stationarity (calendar time vs market state) the strategy should adapt along.

Recent work pushes toward fuller-genome evolution and per-regime specialisation. Zhang et al. (2020) proposed AutoAlpha for hierarchical search and ensemble ranking of alpha factors, applying evolutionary computation to discovering predictive signals rather than optimising fixed trading rules. Long and Kampouridis (2025) developed a genetic algorithm that evolves the *full multi-threshold parameter space* of directional-changes trading strategies — entry, exit, and exposure thresholds simultaneously — confirming the contemporary trend toward complete-genome rather than restricted-subset evolution. Yang et al. (2025) is the closest prior work: a co-evolutionary genetic programming framework with subpopulations evolving independently per market regime, each generating regime-specific alpha factor expressions combined through an HMM-based gating mechanism. The shared principle is per-regime evolutionary isolation; differences are in representation (GP expressions vs fixed-parameter vectors), regime model (HMM-based vs hierarchical GMM), application domain (alpha factor construction vs strategy execution parameters), and migration mechanism (co-evolutionary feedback vs ring-topology sibling migration).

Reinforcement learning has emerged as the dominant alternative paradigm for adaptive trading systems: Liu et al. (2020) introduced FinRL as a deep RL framework, and Bauman et al. (2024) embedded regime-probability estimates into the state representation of a deep RL agent for goal-based investing under regime switching, reporting gains over non-regime-conditioned baselines. The architectural distinction between RL and the evolutionary approach used here is structural — deep RL learns a single policy network mapping state (including regime) to action; the island-model GA maintains explicit, interpretable parameter populations per regime, trading representational capacity for interpretability and per-regime isolation. A direct deep-RL baseline beyond the FinRLPilot Q-learner (§5.4) is identified as future work.

Overfitting remains a persistent challenge regardless of paradigm: evolved or learned solutions may exploit historical noise rather than genuine market structure (Bailey et al., 2014; McLean & Pontiff, 2016). Bailey et al. (2014) demonstrated that the probability of backtest overfitting (PBO) increases rapidly with the number of trials, reaching near-certainty for typical optimisation runs. The methodological framework has since been substantially expanded: Lopez de Prado (2018) develops walk-forward purging and embargo techniques to suppress information leakage in financial cross-validation, and Arian et al. (2024) compare walk-forward, K-fold, purged K-fold, and combinatorial purged cross-validation (CPCV) on synthetic regime-switching data, quantifying the residual PBO under each. This research addresses overfitting through two mechanisms. First, per-regime evolution restricts each population to samples from a single market state, reducing the diversity of conditions each genome must accommodate and thereby reducing the incentive to overfit to transitional patterns. Second, strict hold-out validation: training uses data through December 2024, evaluation begins January 2025. Hold-out is a weaker protocol than CPCV and is sensitive to the specific OOS window; CPCV adaptation to per-regime island populations is identified as future work (§7.7, §8.1).

### 2.3 Island-Model Genetic Algorithms

Island-model GAs maintain semi-isolated subpopulations with periodic migration, preserving diversity by letting different populations explore different regions of the search space (Whitley et al., 1999; Alba & Tomassini, 2002), grounded in Wright's (1931) shifting-balance theory from population genetics. The migration topology — ring, star, random, or problem-specific — controls gene flow and the exploration-exploitation trade-off (Cantu-Paz, 2000). The relevant gap for this work is that topology selection in prior literature is largely empirical: islands are interchangeable subpopulations and the migration graph is chosen heuristically rather than derived from problem structure.

Lopes et al. (2012) proposed a multi-agent approach to dynamically adapt migration topology using Q-learning, demonstrating competitive performance against static ring and random topologies on benchmark optimisation functions. Chideme, Chen, and Lin (2025) introduced three parallel island-model architectures (MSGTSP, IGTSP-Ring, IGTSP-Multikuti) for Group Trading Strategy Portfolio Optimization, achieving computational speed-up ratios of 157% to 287% over sequential methods, with IGTSP-Ring exhibiting the lowest coefficient of variation in high-volatility environments. In both cases, however, the islands serve as parallel search populations on a single shared problem rather than as representations of distinct market states; the topology is adapted or chosen but not *grounded* in problem structure.

The theoretical bridge between the island model and the regime-conditioned approach used here lies in the niching and fitness-sharing literature (Goldberg & Richardson, 1987; Mahfoud, 1995). Niching methods preserve population diversity across distinct peaks of a multimodal fitness landscape; the island model is the spatial analogue, where subpopulation boundaries are explicit rather than implicit. Mahfoud's (1995) treatment is the canonical formalisation of niching methods for genetic algorithms, establishing the conditions under which subdivided populations converge to multiple peaks rather than collapsing to a single global optimum. The conditions are sharpest when the subpopulation boundaries align with structural partitions of the fitness landscape — which is exactly the regime structure the hierarchical GMM discovers. The island-per-regime architecture is therefore a discrete, regime-conditioned form of niching where the subpopulation boundaries are *not* emergent (as in fitness-sharing or crowding) but pre-specified by the regime tree; the regime tree serves the role that the niche-radius parameter plays in fitness-sharing, defining the partition of the fitness landscape that each subpopulation is permitted to specialise in. This connection is rarely made explicit in the EC-for-trading literature, where islands are typically motivated by parallelism and diversity rather than by a niching theorem applied to a known multimodal landscape.

To the best of our knowledge, this research is the first to use market regimes as the structuring principle for island topology in a trading strategy *execution-parameter* optimisation system evaluated under a realistic execution engine. Each island corresponds to a discovered market state, and sibling migration occurs between sub-regimes of the same macro-cluster. The architectural distinction from Yang et al. (2025), the closest prior work in per-regime subpopulation evolution, is detailed in §2.2.

The contribution of this research is the *combination* of four components, each with separate prior art but not previously assembled: (a) hierarchical two-level GMM regime discovery with BIC-based component selection (§3.2), motivated by Orton & Gebbie (2024); (b) island migration topology *derived from* the regime hierarchy itself (§3.3), grounded in Goldberg & Richardson (1987) and Mahfoud (1995) niching theory but applied to a problem-derived rather than heuristic partition; (c) per-regime evolution of the *complete* strategy parameter space — entry signal, direction bias, grid geometry, sizing curve, risk-management controls (§3.4) — extending the full-genome trend (Long & Kampouridis, 2025) with regime conditioning; and (d) fitness evaluation on the *production execution engine* with real per-candle OANDA spread, swap, and slippage costs (§4), addressing the simulation-to-deployment transfer gap (Lopez de Prado, 2018). No prior work integrates all four. The empirical contribution (§6) is the demonstration that this combination collapses the maximum drawdown of a grid-hedged Martingale strategy on EUR-USD by approximately two orders of magnitude under realistic execution costs.

### 2.4 Grid-Hedged Martingale Strategies and Risk Management

Grid-hedged Martingale strategies open additional positions at fixed price intervals against an adverse move, with take-profit targets designed to recover the aggregate position's cost basis (Doob, 1953; Carricaburu, c. 2004); Rundo et al. (2019) introduced the GTSBot grid trading system, one of the few peer-reviewed academic treatments of grid execution on FX and a baseline for the present work (§5). The fundamental property is that these strategies trade a high probability of small wins for a small probability of catastrophic loss. For a grid-hedged variant with maximum depth *N*, geometric multiplier *m*, and per-level loss probability *p*, expected loss upon bust grows as a geometric series in *m* while the probability of reaching successive depths compounds approximately as *p^k*. The critical quantity is the product *p* × *m*: when *p* × *m* < 1.0 the strategy is asymptotically convergent under repeated cycles; when *p* × *m* > 1.0, expected loss diverges with depth and ruin is structurally guaranteed in the limit (Doob, 1953; Ethier, 2010). The bound applies directly to grid-hedged execution on liquid spot instruments where each entry incurs spread cost, which is what makes the EUR-USD-on-OANDA evaluation in §6 a stringent test rather than a contrived one.

Martingale doubling is suboptimal compared to Kelly-style proportional sizing (Kelly, 1956; Thorp, 1969; MacLean, Thorp & Ziemba, 2010): proportional sizing is growth-rate optimal under known probabilities, while doubling exposes the bettor to ruin in finite time even on positive-expectancy games. The *adaptive sizer* in §3.5 borrows from this lineage — its confidence-conditioned base size implements a Kelly-type proportional response under uncertain regime classification, and its drawdown-conditioned shrinkage factor bounds loss under cascading conditions. Yet Martingale-family strategies remain widely deployed in retail and algorithmic forex because per-cycle win rates are high and failures are rare but catastrophic. This combination — high cycle win rates, rare but catastrophic failures, extreme sensitivity to misconfiguration — makes Martingale-family strategies a stringent test case for adaptive risk management rather than a recommendation as a return-generation method.
